mission.title: Install and Configure Kubeclipper on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeClipper is a lightweight web service that provides a user-friendly web console, APIs, and CLI tools for managing Kubernetes clusters. Installing KubeClipper allows you to efficiently deploy and manage Kubernetes clusters across various environments.

steps.title: Add the KubeClipper Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the KubeClipper Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kubeclipper https://charts.kubeclipper.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KubeClipper using Helm
steps.description:
Install KubeClipper with the specified version and create a namespace for it:
```bash
helm install kubeclipper kubeclipper/kubeclipper --version v1.4.1 --namespace kubeclipper --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the KubeClipper pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeclipper
```

steps.title: Expose KubeClipper service
steps.description:
To access the KubeClipper web console, you can port-forward the service:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/kc-server --namespace kubeclipper 8080:80
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for KubeClipper by editing the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kc-server --namespace kubeclipper
``` 
In the editor, ensure the resources section is set as follows:
```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "100Mi"
    cpu: "100m"
  limits:
    memory: "500Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeClipper will show the KubeClipper pods running in the 'kubeclipper' namespace. You can access the web console by navigating to http://localhost:8080 in your web browser.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubeclipper https://charts.kubeclipper.io
helm repo update
helm install kubeclipper kubeclipper/kubeclipper --version v1.4.1 --namespace kubeclipper --create-namespace

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To remove the KubeClipper release, execute the following command to uninstall it from the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall kubeclipper --namespace kubeclipper
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the release, clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes that were created. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd kubeclippers.kubeclipper.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kubeclipper
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace used for KubeClipper and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubeclipper
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your KubeClipper installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kubeclipper -o yaml > kubeclipper-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the new version of KubeClipper:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubeclipper kubeclipper/kubeclipper --version v1.5.0 --namespace kubeclipper
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the KubeClipper pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeclipper
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the KubeClipper pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubeclipper
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the KubeClipper service, ensure that the service is running and check the port-forwarding command:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kubeclipper
``` 
If the service is not running, you may need to restart the pods or check the deployment configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeClipper fails to start due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kc-server --namespace kubeclipper
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for any conflicting resources or version compatibility issues. You can view the error message in the terminal. To rollback to the previous version, use:
```bash
helm rollback kubeclipper --namespace kubeclipper
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed on your local machine.
